frameworks.name: efficient coding
frameworks.one_line: Neural encoding accuracy should track adaptation-induced stimulus statistics.

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: Discrimination thresholds inverted via Cramer-Rao bound to recover encoding accuracy.

frameworks.name: sensory adaptation
frameworks.one_line: Prolonged exposure reshapes orientation representation and behavior.

core_question: How does prolonged orientation adaptation reshape neural encoding accuracy across the orientation space, and is the change consistent with efficient coding?
key_assumptions: Psychophysical thresholds bound underlying neural encoding accuracy via Cramer-Rao.
Efficient coding predicts encoding accuracy tracks adaptation stimulus statistics.
Control adaptor matched in non-orientation aspects isolates orientation-specific effects.
manipulations: Adaptor type: oblique 45 deg grating vs non-oriented control adaptor.
Test orientation relative to adaptor (at, perpendicular, intermediate).
key_findings: Thresholds at the adaptor orientation dropped substantially after adaptation.
Thresholds perpendicular to the adaptor also dropped substantially.
Encoding accuracy increased at and perpendicular to the adaptor, decreased in between.
Increase at the adaptor matches the relative stimulus frequency increase during adaptation.
interpretations: Encoding change at the adaptor agrees with the efficient coding hypothesis.
Perpendicular accuracy gain is not explained by efficient coding alone.
limitations_noted: Decoding side (whether the system is aware of adaptation-induced encoding changes) left open.
